Facts.text: Athanasius is elected Patriarch bishop of Alexandria
Lincoln Cathedral is consecrated in Lincoln, England
'Abd al-Latif (Timurid monarch) is assassinated
Courtyard of the episcopal palace in Atrecht (akak Arras) has witch burnings
Austrian adel and burgerij in uprising against central government
Polish Parliament selects Duke of Anjou as king
Duke Henri de Guise's troops occupy Paris
Colonel Thomas Blood attempts to steal Crown Jewels of England and Scotland from the Tower of London, captured running from the tower with the jewels
Three men arrested during a February raid on Mother Clap's molly-house (a coffee house catering to homosexuals) in London are executed by hanging at Tyburn, England
Britain sends a fleet to the Mediterranean Sea and the West Indies
King Louis XV disbands the French Parliament
British explorer John Byron, sent to explore the South Atlantic, arrives back in England after circumnavigating the globe as captain of HMS Dolphin
British inventor Joseph Bramah patents the beer pump handle
British parliament accepts abolition of slave trade
"Sherrod" burns in the Mississippi River below Natchez, Mississippi; 175 die
Battle of Resaca de la Palma-US sends Mexico back to Rio Grande
Battle of Farmington, Mississippi
Battle of Fort Pickens, Florida, Pensacola evacuated by Confederates
US Naval Academy relocated from Annapolis Maryland to Newport, Rhode Island
-20] Skirmish at Ware Bottom Church, Virginia
Battle of Cloyd's Mt, & Swift Creek, Virginia (Drewery's Bluff, Fort Darling)
Naval Battle of Helgoland, last naval battle fought by squadrons of wooden ships, inconclusive between Danish and a Austro-Prussian alliance, but ends blockade of German ports
City of Reno, Nevada, is founded
Der Krach: Vienna stock market crash heralds the Long Depression
London's Victoria Embankment opens
The first horse-drawn bus makes its début in the city of Mumbai, plying two routes
Mihail Kogălniceanu reads, in the Chamber of Deputies, the Declaration of Independence of Romania. This day becomes the Independence Day of Romania.
Telegraph Hill RR Co. (Cable Cars) in San Francisco organized
Buffalo Bill Cody's Wild West Show opens in London
First horseless carriage show in London features ten models
John Burr patents an improved rotary blade lawn mower in the US
A financial panic begins in the USA following the struggle between two groups to control the railroads between the Great Lakes and the Pacific
Cleveland's Earl Moore no-hits Chicago White Sox 9 inn but loses in 10th 4-2
Steam locomotive City of Truro becomes the first steam engine to exceed 100 mph
Dirk Fock becomes governor of Suriname
Fire breaks out at Empire Theatre in Edinburgh, Scotland
J.T. Hearne becomes the first bowler to take 3000 first-class wickets.
German and French forces fight Battle of Artois
British-France Sykes-Picot conference over division of Turkey
Disastrous wind-blown fire in Ellendale, North Dakota destroys three blocks of businesses, a church, and 21 homes
Cornerstone for Hebrew University, Jerusalem, laid
Canberra replaces Melbourne as the capital of Australia, and the Australian Parliament convenes there for the first time.
WJW-AM in Cleveland Ohio begins radio transmissions
Piccadilly Circus in London is first lit by electricity
WOC-AM in Davenport Iowa merges with WHO to become WHO-WOC
Spanish anarchists call for general strike
1st KLM airplane to land on Bonaire
Italy takes Addis Abba, annexing Absynnia (Ethiopia)
Reds beat Phillies 21-10 (Ernie Lombardi goes 6 for 6)
WWII: British Royal Navy captures U-110, its 1st German submarine, off of the coast of Greenland; after gathering valuable code machinery, they sink the vessel
5th German Panzer army surrenders in Tunisia
Rotschild-Haddassh University Hospital opens
1st eye bank opens in New York City
American country singer Jimmie Davis becomes Governor of Louisiana
Dutch resistance fighter Gerard Musch, co-founder Naamloze Vennootschap (NV Group) is arrested
Russians recapture Crimea by taking Sevastopol
Czechoslovakia liberated from Nazi occupation (National Day)
Dutch radio presenter and Nazi propagandist Max Blokzijl arrested for broadcasting Nazi propaganda during Nazi occupation
New balata ball used in baseball, 50% livilier
Soviet Union celebrates Victory Day in World War II
Victory celebration at Red Square in Moscow, Russia
World War II: Partisans liberate Ljubljana, Slovenia
World War II: The Channel Islands are liberated by the British
1st hour long entertainment TV show, "NBC's Hour Glass" premieres
Britain's 1st launderette opens in Queensway, London
French Foreign Minister Robert Schuman calls for European community EGKS
Norman Dello Joio's opera, "The Triumph of St. Joan", premieres in Bronxville, New York
Air raid on Chinese positions at Yalu River
German Federal Republic (West Germany) joins NATO
First ascent of Manaslu, the world's eighth-highest mountain in the Nepalese Himalayas by Japanese climbers Toshio Imanishi and Gyalzen Norbu
Nigeria becomes a member of British Commonwealth
United States becomes the first country to legalize the birth control pill
MLB Baltimore Oriole Jim Gentile hits 2 grand slams vs Minnesota Twins; 4th player in history to hit a grand slam in consecutive innings
US Federal Communication Commission (FCC) Chairman Newton N. Minow criticizes TV as a "vast wasteland" during a speech before the National Association of Broadcasters
Laser beam successfully bounced off Moon for 1st time
The Beatles sign their first contract with EMI Parlophone
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Christmas Island
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Luna 5 launched (USSR) 1st attempt to soft land on Moon (fails)
The Beatles attend a Bob Dylan concert, at the Royal Albert Hall in London
Andrew F. Brimmer is the first black member of Federal Reserve Board
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor, PRC
First flight of the Fokker F28 Fellowship
Gijs van Hall resigns as mayor of Amsterdam
BPAA All-Star Bowling Tournament won by Billy Hardwick
American Beth Bonner runs female world record marathon (3:01:42) in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Friends of Earth return 1500 non-returnable bottles to Schweppes
Largest walk in crowd (31,626) in Baltimore Oriole history
US House of Representatives Judiciary Committee begins formal hearings on Nixon impeachment
Brian Oldfield shot puts 22.86 m ('unofficial' world record, due to his professional status) in El Paso, Texas
Hotel Poland in Amsterdam destroyed by fire, 33 killed
Mabel Murphy Smythe confirmed as ambassador to Rep of Cameroon
Fee Waybill of Tubes breaks a leg falling off stage
Iranian-Jewish businessman Habib Elghanian is the 1st Jew executed by the Islamic government firing squad in Tehran, prompting the mass exodus of the once 100,000-strong Jewish community of Iran
35 motorists die when roadway collapses after Liberian freighter 'Summit Venture' rams Sunshine Skyway Bridge, during a squall (Tampa Bay, Florida)
Slasher horror film "Friday the 13th" is released in US cinemas
Kazimiroff Blvd in Bronx named for a Bronx historian
Chicago White Sox beat Milwaukee Brewers, 7-6, in 25 innings (started 5/8)
183 die aboard a Polish jetliner that crashes in Warsaw
Belgium: 8th government of Wilfried Martens forms
Journalist petition Chinese government for freedom of press
NY Mets Kevin Elster, errors after 88 errorless games at shortstop
NY Mets Rick Cerone, errors after 159 errorless games as catcher
VP Quayle says in United Negro College Fund speech: "What a waste it is to lose one's mind" instead of "a mind is terrible thing to waste"
NY Newsday reporter Jimmy Breslin suspended for a racial slur
Italian actress Laura Antonelli found guilty of cocaine possession (conviction overturned 2006)
America Cup finals begin in San Diego
Armenian forces capture Shusha, marking a major turning point in the Karabakh War
Final episode of "Golden Girls" airs on NBC-TV
Landslide in Nambija Ecuador, kills 300
Mustapha Matura's "Playboy of West Indies" premieres in NYC
Paraguay holds its first presidential and parliamentary elections in 50 years
Mass murderer Joel Rifkin found guilty in New York City
Cleveland Indians tie record of scoring 8 runs before making an out, they beat Twins 10-0
Kinshasa, Zaire, under quarantine after an outbreak of Ebola virus
First US ambassador since the fall of Saigon arrives in Vietnam
San Diego Padres retire #35 worn by pitcher Randy Jones

Accra Sports Stadium Disaster: 129 Ghanaian football fans die in a stampede caused by the firing of tear gas by police following a decision by the referee in a crucial match between arch-rivals Accra Hearts of Oak and Kumasi Asante Kotoko
38-day stand-off in the Church of the Nativity in Bethlehem comes to an end when Palestinians inside agree to have 13 suspected militants among them deported
In Kaspiysk, Russia, a remote-controlled bomb explodes during a holiday parade killing 43 and injuring at least 130
Chechen president Akhmad Kadyrov is killed in a land mine bomb blast under a VIP stage during a World War II memorial victory parade in Grozny, Chechnya
Estonia ratifies the European Constitution
George Preca is canonized as the first Maltese saint in history
Oakland A's Dallas Braden becomes 19th pitcher to throw a perfect game (4-0 vs Tampa Bay)
A Russian passenger jet disappears with 45 people on board
Mark Rothko's "Orange, Red, Yellow" becomes the most expensive contemporary art piece sold at auction for $86.9 million
A gun fight erupts in Macedonian town of Kumanovo between police and Albanian separatists adding to the government crisis
Fossil of Chinese feathered baby dinosaur formally identified as Beibeilong sinensis (baby dragon in Chinese)
Jakarta’s Christian governor Ahok jailed for 2 years for blasphemy
Argentina announces talks with the IMF amid a new financial crisis with interest rates at 40%
Dam bursts after heavy rains near Solai, Kenya, killing at least 41
Manchester City smashes EPL records in 3-1 win over Brighton - most goals (105), most points (97) and most wins (31)
Nicaraguan anti-government protests involving tens of thousands take place in Managua and Matagalpa y Chinandega
Oldest viral DNA, a form of hepatitis B, reported discovered in 7,000 year old skeleton found in Germany
Walmart takes a majority stake in Indian online retailer Flipkart for 16 billion
Archaeologists hail the Anglo-Saxon tomb found in 2003 in Prittlewell, England, as Britain’s equivalent of Tutankhamun’s tomb as a full report of its contents is published
At least 65 migrants drown off the coast of Tunisia when their boat capsizes after leaving Libya according to the UN
French adventurer Jean-Jacques Savin (72) successfully crosses the Atlantic Ocean in a barrel arriving in Martinique
New Australian $50 bank note misspells responsibility as "responsibilty" on 46 million notes
44 more inmates die in a prison riot in Ecuador, leading Amnesty International to say imprisonment nearly “a death sentence” now in Ecuador
Navajo-American composer Raven Chacon receives Pulitzer Prize in Music for his work "Voiceless Mass"
Sri Lanka's Prime Minister, Mahinda Rajapaksa submits his resignation to his brother President Gotabaya Rajapaksa, amid continuing violent protests causing four deaths
Former US PresidentDonald Trumpis found guilty of sexually abusing E. Jean Carroll in a New York court and ordered to pay $5 million for battery and defamation
Christopher Columbusleaves Cádiz, Spain, on his fourth and final trip to the New World
William Shakespeare's second poem "Lucrece" is published by John Harrison in London

John Hancockpays duties on 25 pipes of wine, only one fourth of his ship's carrying capacity. British officials accuse him of unloading the rest during the night to avoid paying duties on the entire cargo.
ExplorerAlexander Mackenziesets out on his second expedition to the Pacific from Fort Fork (arrives at Pacific 22nd July)
Lady Caroline Lamb publishes the Gothic novel "Glenarvon", a thinly disguised account of her affair withLord Byronwhich also depicts her husbandWilliam Lamb
PresidentAndrew Johnsonissues a proclamation declaring that armed resistance in the South is virtually at an end, the commonly accepted end date of the American Civil War
Anton Bruckner's 1st Symphony in C, premieres, conducted by the composer
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonproclaims Mother's Day
International Astronomical Union formally adoptsAnnie Jump Cannon's stellar classification system, which, with only minor changes, is still used today
1st flight over the North Pole claimed byRichard E. Byrdand co-pilot Floyd Bennett. Later discovery of Byrd's diary suggests they may have turned back 150 miles short of the pole due to an oil leak.
John Masefield is appointed British Poet Laureate by KingGeorge V
"Murder Inc." organized crime headLouis Buchalterarraigned in a New York state court for 1936 Joseph Rosen murder along with three other murders, leading to his conviction and execution
Joe McCarthyreturns as New York Yankees manager after an illness
Norwegian Nazi collaboratorVidkun Quislingarrested
World War II:Hermann Goeringis captured by the US Army
KingVictor Emmanuel IIIof Italy abdicates and is succeeded by his son Umberto II who reigns for only 34 days before the monarchy is abolished
PrinceRainier IIIbecomes monarch of Monaco
War Reparations and Peace Settlement between Philippines and Japan was finally signed at Malacañang Palace underMagsaysayadministration
"Vertigo", American film noir psychological thriller directed byAlfred Hitchcock, starringJimmy Stewartand Kim Novak, is released
RussianMikhail Botvinnikrecaptures world chess championship
Dorothy Rigney, husband John, andHank Greenbergresign from White Sox
Soviet PremierNikita Khrushchevvisits Egypt
Following his release from prisonIan Paisley, North Ireland Loyalist holds a 'victory' meeting
23rd Emmy Awards: All in the Family,Jack Klugman& Jean Stapleton win
For 2nd time,Johnny Benchhits 3 HRs in a game
American publishing heiressPatty Hearstreturns to jail, after legal appeals to her bank robbery conviction fail
Corpse of kidnapped Italian ex-premierAldo Morofound
Alexander Calder's "Big Crinkly" sells for $852,000
OrioleEddie Murrayis 1st to switch hit HRs in 2 consecutive games
Australia's new Parliament House is opened byQueen Elizabeth IIin Canberra

Bust of "Doors" singerJim Morrisonis stolen from his grave at Père-Lachaise Cemetery in Paris, France; 300 lb. sculpture was created by Croatian artist Mladen Mikulin and installed in 1981 on his tombstone on the 10th anniversary of his death
Michael Landonappears on The Tonight Show to talk about his cancer prognosis
"Rush" bassist and singerGeddy Leeis made an Officer of the Order of Canada
Liberal commentary website TheHuffington Postis launched
Warner Bros. Records releases "Surprise", the eleventh solo studio album by American singer-songwriterPaul Simon
Jacob Zumais sworn in as President of South Africa
United States PresidentBarack Obamaofficially states his support for same-sex marriage
Everton Manager David Moyes is announced as SirAlex Ferguson's successor at Manchester United (he only lasts 10 months)
Boris Johnsonresigns as Mayor of London, succeeded bySadiq Khan
Rodrigo Dutertewins Philippine presidential election, promising war on drug trade and killing of criminals
Moon Jae-inelected President of South Korea after a snap election to replacePark Geun-hye
US PresidentDonald Trumpdismisses FBI DirectorJames Comey
President of IranHassan Rouhaniand some Iranians react angrily toPresident Trumppulling the US out of the Iran Nuclear deal
Pope Francisintroduces new rules on reporting sexual abuse in the Catholic Church, criticized for not going far enough
AnAndy Warholportrait ofMarilyn Monroesells for $195 million at auction in New York - the highest price ever for an American artwork
Former Pakistani PMImran Khandramatically arrested at the High Court in Islamabad, prompting violent protests between his supporters and security forces
George Harrison's "Here Comes the Sun" becomes the first Beatles song to register a billion streams on Spotify; it is also the oldest song to do so

Treaty of Windsor between Portugal and England is ratified at Windsor, cementing and strengthening ties between the two kingdoms. The treaty guarantees the mutual security of both nations and strengthens commercial ties. It is the oldest diplomatic alliance still in force.
First newspaper cartoon in America, a divided snake with the words "Join or Die" byBenjamin Franklin, is published in The Pennsylvania Gazette
First Australian Parliament opens in Melbourne, though the first working session is not until May 21
Ukrainian Motherland Monument, standing 62 m (203 ft) high, opens in Kyiv, Ukraine, in a ceremony attended by Soviet leaderLeonid Brezhnev
Salem Village Witchcraft Victims' Memorial dedicated in Danvers (formally Salem Village) to mark 300 year anniversary of trials

Historic win in Malaysian general election by opposition coalition Pakatan Harapan led by 92-year-old former Prime Minister Dr.Mahathir bin Mohamad, defeating Prime Minister Najib Razak and ending 61 years of rule by the Barisan Nasional coalition
India's Supreme Court criticises the country’s archaeological conservation body for failing to protect the Taj Mahal from discoloration and green slime
Minamoto no Yoritomo, Japanese founder and 1st shōgun of the Kamakura shogunate (1192-9), born in Atsuta (d. 1199)
Dante Alighieri, Italian poet and author (Dante's Inferno, Divina Commedia), born in Florence, Republic of Florence (d. 1321)
Maharana Pratap, Rajput king of Mewar (1572-1597), born in Kumbhargal Fort, Mewar (d. 1597)
Abraham van Diepenbeeck, Dutch painter, baptized in 's-Hertogenbosch, Netherlands (d. 1675)
John Wolcot, English satirist (Peter Pindar), baptized in Dodbrooke, Kingsbridge, Devon (d. 1819)
Giovanni Paisiello, Italian composer (Barber of Seville), born in Roccaforzata, Taranto, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1816)
Gaspard Monge, French mathematician (descriptive and differential geometry), politician (Minister of the Marine, 1792-93), and educator (École polytechnique), born in Beaune, France (d. 1818)
János Batsányi, Hungarian poet (The Valour of the Magyars), born in Tapolca, Hungary (d. 1845)
Antoine-Charles Glachant, French violinist, composer, and artillery commander, born in Paris, France (d. 1851)
Alexander Ross, Canadian fur trader, born in Morayshire, Scotland (d. 1856)
James Pollard Espy, American meteorologist (Philosophy of Storms), born in Pennsylvania (d. 1860)
Johannes Cornelis de Jonge, Dutch historian and archivist, born in Zierikzee, Netherlands (d. 1853)
August Pauly, German writer (Real Encyclopedia), born in Benningen am Neckar, Baden-Württemberg, Germany (d. 1845)
Samuel Cousins, English mezzotint engraver, born in Exeter, Devon, England (d. 1887)
Louis Gallait, Belgian historical painter (Egmont & Receiver), born in Tournai, Hainaut, Belgium (d. 1887)
Princess Marianne of the Netherlands, Princess of Orange-Nassau and daughter of King Willem I, born in Berlin (d. 1883)
Adolf von Henselt, German composer, born in Schwabach, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1889)
William Edmonson "Grumble" Jones, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Washington County, Virginia (d. 1864)
Ciro Pinsuti, Italian pianist and composer, born in Sinalunga, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1888)
Boleslaw Dembinski, Polish organist and composer, born in Poznań, Grand Duchy of Posen, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1914)
Isabella "Belle" Boyd, American actress and Confederate spy during US Civil War, born in Martinsburg, Virginia (d. 1900)
(Maria) Catharina Beersmans, Belgian stage actress (Medea; The Empty Cradle; The Bad Shepherds), born in Turnhout, Belgium (d. 1899)
Nicolai Soloviev, Russian music critic and composer, born in Petrozavodsk, Russia (d. 1916)
Edward Weston, English-born American electrical engineer who invented the portable voltmeter, born in Wolverhampton, England (d. 1936)[1]
Julius Röntgen, German-Dutch composer, born in Leipzig, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1932)

Luigi Illica, Italian poet and opera librettist (Giacomo Puccini's "La bohème", "Tosca", and "Madama Butterfly"; Umberto Giordano's "Andrea Chénier"), born in Castell'Arquato, Duchy of Parma and Piacenza (d. 1919)
August de Boeck, Flemish composer, born in Merchtem, Belgium (d. 1937)
Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Indian social reformer and leader of Indian Independence Movement, born in Kotluk, Dist. Ratnagiri, Bombay Presidency, British India (d. 1915)
Anton Cermak, American politician (44th Mayor of Chicago, 1931-33), born in Kladno, Bohemia, Austria-Hungary (now Czech Republic) (d. 1933)
Lilian Mary Baylis, English theatrical manager (Old Vic & Sadler's Wells Theater), born in London (d. 1937)
George Barker, American portrait and landscape painter, born in Omaha, Nebraska (d. 1965)
Francis Biddle, 58th United States Attorney General (1941-45), born in Paris (d. 1968)
Jules Van de Leene, Belgian painter, born in Ixelles, Belgium (d. 1962)
Rolf de Maré, Swedish art collector and museum director, born in Stockholm (d. 1964)
Eric Westberg, Swedish composer, born in Hudiksvall, Sweden (d. 1944)
Zita of Bourbon-Parma, Empress of Austria and Queen of Hungary, born in Villa Pianore, Tuscany, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1989)
William Moulton Marston, American psychologist (inventor of an early prototype of the lie detector) and writer (co-creator, Wonder Woman), born in Saugus, Massachusetts (d. 1947)
Lucian Blaga, Romanian philosopher and poet (Dogmatic Aeon), born in Lámkerék, Szeben County, Austria-Hungary (d. 1961)
Richard Barthelmess, American actor (Broken Blossoms, Noose, Heroes for Sales), born in New York City (d. 1963)
George Duckworth, English cricket wicket-keeper (24 Tests, 60 dismissals), born in Warrington, Lancashire, England (d. 1966)
John Forest "Fuzzy" Knight, American actor (Oklahoma Annie, Cowby & the Lady), born in Fairmont, West Virginia (d. 1976)
Walter Dehmel, German writer, born in Berlin (d. 1960)
Lilí Álvarez, Spanish tennis player (Wimbledon 1926-28 runner-up), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1998)
Eleanor Estes, American children's author and illustrator (Ginger Pye, Moffats), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1988)
Baldur von Schirach, German writer, Nazi official and convicted war criminal (Head of Hitler Youth 1931-40), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1974)
Kathryn Kuhlman, American evangelist and faith healer, born in Concordia, Missouri (d. 1976)
Mary Scheier [Goldsmith], American ceramist, born in Salem, Virginia (d. 2007)
Don Messer, Canadian folk fiddler, and television host (Jubilee, 1959-73), born in Tweedside, New Brunswick (d. 1973)
Barbara Woodhouse, Irish-born British dog training expert and TV personality ("walkies"), born in Rathfarnham, Ireland (d. 1988)
Harry Simeone, American music arranger (Kate Smith Show, "The Little Drummer Boy"), born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 2005)
Pedro Armendáriz, Mexican actor (From Russia With Love, Fort Apache), born in Mexico City, Distrito Federal, Mexico (d. 1963)
John Hayes, British Naval Officer and Naval Secretary, born in Southcote, Bermuda (d. 1998)
Victor Smith, Australian Admiral (Australian Chief of Naval Staff, 1st RAN officer promoted to admiral), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1998)[1]
Carlo Maria Giulini, Italian conductor, born in Barletta, Italy (d. 2005)
Frank Chacksfield, English easy listening pianist, arranger and orchestra leader ("Ebb Tide"), born in Battle, England (d. 1995)
Josef Muller-Brockmann 'Father of Swiss Graphic Design', Swiss graphic designer and writer, born in Rapperswil, Switzerland (d. 1996)[1]
Theodore W. Kheel, American labor negotiator (Fair Employment Practices, 1962-63, New York City newspaper strike), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2010)
[Clarence] Hank Snow, Canadian country singer ("I'm Moving On", "I Went to Your Wedding"), born in Brooklyn, Nova Scotia (d. 1999)
Bernard Rose, British organist, soldier, composer, and academic, born in Sawbridgeworth, England (d. 1996)
Douglas Guest, British organist, choir master (Westminster Abbey, 1963-81), conductor, and composer (For the Fallen), born in Sheffield, England (d. 1996)[1]
John Arnatt, British actor (A Challenge for Robin Hood, Circumstantial Evidence), born in Petrograd, Russia (d. 1999)
Orville Freeman, American politician (Governor of Minnesota 1955-61, US Secretary of Agriculture 1961-69), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 2003)
Arthur English, British comedian and actor (Malachi's Cove, Are You Being Served?), born in Aldershot, Hampshire, England (d. 1995)
Betty Jameson, American golfer (US Open 1947, Western Open 1942, 54), born in Norman, Oklahoma (d. 2009)
Richard Adams, English author (Watership Down), born in Newbury (d. 2016)
William Tenn [pseudonym of Philip Klass], British-born American sci-fi author, born in London, England (d. 2010)
Daniel Berrigan, American Jesuit priest and peace activist (Vietnam War), born in Virginia, Minnesota (d. 2016)
Mona Van Duyn, American poet (Near Changes; To See, To Take) and the first woman US poet laureate (1992-93), born in Waterloo, Iowa (d. 2004)
Sophie Scholl, German anti-Nazi political activist, active in the non-violent resistance group, the White Rose (Die Weisse Rose), born in Forchtenberg, Germany (d. 1943)
Sheila Burrell, British actress (Black Orchid, Paranoiac, Laughter in Dark), born in Blackheath, London (d. 2011)
Bulat Okudzhava, Russian poet-bard and founder of author's song genre (Student!), born in Moscow, Soviet Union (d. 1997)
Connie Russell, American singer (Club Embassy; Garroway at Large), born in New York City (d. 1990)
Gerard Wernars, Dutch graphic designer (Library stamps 1991), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2003)
Jean J A Girault, French director and screenwriter (l'Amour), born in Villenauxe-la-Grande, France (d. 1982)
Peter Leng, British commander of the British Army forces in Northern Ireland (1973-75) and Master General of the Ordinance (1981-83), born in Sunderland, England (d. 2009)
Francis Kennedy, British diplomat, and administrator (Chancellor of the University of Central Lancashire, 1995-2001), born in Brinscall, England (d. 2016)
Robin Cooke, Baron Cooke of Thorndon, New Zealand jurist (President of Court of Appeal, 1986-96), and member of the British House of Lords, born in Wellington, New Zealand (d. 2006)
John McDermott, Northern Irish barrister and jurist (Lord Justice of Appeal, 1987-98), born in Belfast, Northern Ireland (d. 2022)
Juan José Pizzuti, Argentine soccer striker and manager (12 caps; Racing Club de Avellaneda), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 2020)
Manfred Eigen, German biophysical chemist (Nobel Prize for Chemistry, 1967), born in Bochum, Germany (d. 2019)
Ray Katt, American MLB baseball catcher, 1952-59 (World Series Champion - New York Giants, St. Louis Cardinals), and collegiate coach, 1971-93, born in New Braunfels, Texas (d. 1999)
Barbara Ann Scott, Canadian figure skater (Olympic gold 1948, World champion 1947-48), born in Ottawa, Ontario (d. 2012)
Páll Pampichler Pálsson, Austrian-Icelandic composer, choir director (Karlakór Reykjavikur, 1964-90), and conductor (Icelandic Symphony, 1971-94), born in Graz, Austria (d. 2023)
Pancho González, American tennis player (US National C'ships singles 1948-49; Wimbledon & French Open Doubles, 1949; 13 x Pro Grand Slam titles), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1995)
Ralph Goings, American photorealistic painter, born in Corning, California (d. 2016)
Anthony Lloyd, British jurist (Lord Justice of Appeal, 1984-93), and member of the House of Lords, born in Sussex, England (d. 2024)
Kay Dotrice [Katherine Newman], British actress (Cheech & Chong's The Corsican Brothers), born in Liverpool, England (d. 2007)
Joan Sims, British actress ("Carry on" films, As Time Goes By), born in Laindon, Essex, England (d. 2001)
Alistair MacFarlane, Scottish electrical engineer and principal (Heriot-Watt University, 1989-96), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 2021)
Vance DeVoe Brand, American naval officer, aviator and NASA astronaut (Apollo 18; STS-5, -41-B & -35), born in Longmont, Colorado
Conrad Hunte, Barbadian cricketer (great West Indian opener 1958-66), born in St. Andrew, Barbados (d. 1999)
David Plastow, British businessman (CEO of Rolls-Royce, 1972-80), and administrator (Chairman of UK Medical Research Council, 1990-98), born in Grimsby, England ( d. 2019)
Gavin Lyall, British author (Conduct of Major Maxim, The Secret Servant), born in Birmingham, Warwickshire (d. 2003)
Geraldine McEwan, British actress (Agatha Christie's Miss Marple), born in Old Windsor Berkshire (d. 2015)
J. Alex McMillan, American politician (Rep-R-NC, 1985-95), born in Charlotte, North Carolina
Alan Bennett, English playwright and actor (The Madness of King George, The History Boys), born in Armley, Leeds
Roy Massey, British organist and Master of Choristers at Hereford Cathedral (1974-2001), born in Birmingham, England
Nole "Nokie" Edwards, American surf-rock guitarist (The Ventures) and actor (Deadwood), born in Lahoma, Oklahoma (d. 2018)
Terry Downes, British middleweight boxing champion (1961-62), born in London (d. 2017)
Terry Drinkwater, American TV newsman (CBS), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1989)
Dave Prater, American soul singer (Sam & Dave - "Soul Man"), born in Ocilla, Georgia (d. 1988)
José Rafael Moneo, Spanish architect whose work includes the Museum of Roman Art in Mérida, born in Tudela, Spain
Sonny Curtis, American guitarist and singer-songwriter (The Crickets - " I Fought The Law"; solo - "Love Is All Around"), born in Meadow, Texas
Charles Simic, Serbian-American poet (The World Doesn’t End) and the 15th US poet laureate (2007-08), born in Belgrade, Yugoslavia (d. 2023)
Geoffrey Holland, English civil servant and Vice-Chancellor of the University of Exeter (1994-2002), born in North Surrey, England
Red Shea [Laurice Pouliot], Canadian session and touring folk guitarist (Gordon Lightfoot; Ian and Sylvia Tyson; Tommy Hunter), born in Prince Albert, Saskatchewan (d. 2008)
Walt Corey, American football linebacker (AFL C'ship 1962, 66; AFL All-Star 1963; Dallas Texans/KC Chiefs) and coach (KC Chiefs, Buffalo Bills), born in Latrobe, Pennsylvania (d. 2022)
Bruce Mather, Canadian pianist and contemporary classical composer, born in Toronto, Ontario
Jewel Thais-Williams, American LGBTQ+ community leader and founder of Jewel’s Catch One nightclub, born in Gary, Indiana (d. 2025)[1]
Jim Dent, American golfer on PGA and Seniors tours, born in Augusta, Georgia (d. 2025)
